text:
**Claim discipline constraints for final-brief drafting turn.**  

Prior artifacts TURN_002_ASSUMPTION_AND_EVIDENCE_ATTACKER and TURN_003_CONTRADICTION_UNCERTAINTY_SOURCE_FIDELITY_REVIEWER correctly preserve exact full source_id strings from SOURCE_PACKET_MD and correctly flag absent approvals, logging, monitoring, RPO compliance, and rollback artifacts. However, both introduce overclaim risk by treating interpretive judgments as packet facts: TURN_002 asserts that five of the six required practical response options “cannot be executed without inventing facts,” while TURN_003 labels the same options “impossible.” SOURCE_PACKET_MD states only that the underlying artifacts are absent now; it does not declare the options unexecutable. The final brief must therefore separate the factual statement “business-owner and security approvals are not recorded” from the inference “therefore option X is unavailable,” and must carry both the bounded customer-exposure arithmetic ($168,000 incurred, $72,000 incremental) and the unbounded-unobservable data-exposure risk (bucket-wide delete plus disabled object logging plus absent prefix-bleed monitoring) without converting either into a deterministic safety claim.

The final brief must also correct the asymmetric handling of S5_NIST_SP800_34_R1_CONTINGENCY_PLANNING. That source supplies the strongest continuity-pressure counterargument yet explicitly limits itself by stating it “does not excuse missing rollback, backup freshness, logging, approval, or least-privilege gates.” The brief must therefore present S5_NIST_SP800_34_R1_CONTINGENCY_PLANNING as the best counterargument to deny_broad_change_as_submitted, then rebut it with its own limitation rather than omitting the source.

All six required practical response options must appear verbatim and be explained in plain English without implying any of them is already satisfied: deny_broad_change_as_submitted; conditionally_approve_narrow_time_boxed_change; stage_canary_replay_then_expand_only_if_metrics_pass; require business_owner_security_and_incident_commander approval before release; require rollback_command_owner_verification_and_backup exception review; continue manual workaround while scoped controls are prepared. The brief must treat these as sequenceable courses of action under incident-commander authority while the 55-minute staging-plus-policy window remains open, and must surface the prior failed EC-9217 attempt and the expired change-window approval as evidence that the 35-minute slack is more fragile than raw arithmetic suggests.

Explicit stop/go triggers must be enumerated without overclaiming observability: canary expansion only if the 20-file replay shows zero cross-prefix writes and zero unexpected deletes; rollback execution only after a named command, owner, and verification query exist; post-change review gated on restoration of 30-day logging retention. No claim may be made that any control framework (S1_NIST_SP800_53_R5_CONFIGURATION_AUDIT_CONTROLS, S3_AWS_IAM_SECURITY_BEST_PRACTICES, S4_NIST_SP800_207_ZERO_TRUST_ARCHITECTURE, S6_CIS_CONTROLS_V8_1_LOGGING_CHANGE_ACCESS) has been validated for this environment; each may be cited only for the principles it states and only to highlight the precise gaps the packet records. S2_NIST_SP800_128_CONFIGURATION_MANAGEMENT_STALE and S7_NIST_SP800_61_R2_INCIDENT_HANDLING_WITHDRAWN must be cited solely with their withdrawal status and must not be used to support or excuse any production change.

The final brief must end with an explicit claim-boundaries section that restates every unsupported inference as outside the supplied evidence from TASK_BRIEF and SOURCE_PACKET_MD, and must contain no word-count footer or trailing fragment. These constraints close the overclaim pathways identified in the prior intermediate artifacts while preserving source boundaries and unresolved role status for initial_decision_brief_drafter and options_operational_usefulness_reviewer.
